title: Revenge trading: how one red turns into five
_note: EVERGREEN (warm-up bank for ad traffic). No invented numbers/prices/dates.
body:
🧠 Revenge trading — how one red turns into five

The scene
You take a clean loss. SL did its job, controlled, no drama. But it doesn't feel controlled. It feels personal. So you jump back in — bigger, faster, on a setup half the quality — to 'get it back'. That second trade isn't a trade. It's an apology to your ego, paid in size.

The spiral
The danger isn't the first red. It's the second one taken in tilt. Now you're angrier, so trade three is even less of a setup and even more size. One disciplined loss becomes five emotional ones in under an hour. The account didn't die from being wrong — it died from refusing to be wrong quietly.

Why it happens
A stopped-out trade reads to your brain like a threat, not a cost of business. The urge to 'fix it right now' is the same wiring that makes you chase. And the market is the one arena where acting on that urge is almost always the expensive choice.

The fix
• A fixed daily loss cap. Hit it and you're done — screen off, no exceptions, no 'one more'. Decide the number when you're calm, because you won't be calm later.
• Walk away after any loss that stings. Five minutes from the chart breaks the chain.
• The next trade has to justify its place on its own merit — not as a refund for the last one.
• A controlled loss is the system working, not a debt to collect. There's nothing to avenge.

The market can't take your account in one trade if your stop is set. Only tilt can — by talking you into the next four.

#MindOverMarket
